turns.text: Hello Cherie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s nice to meet you. I guess I’m ready, though "ready" is a funny word for how I feel these days. I’m sitting here looking at those old mills across the street, just watching the light shift, and it’s hard to believe this is my life now. I waited so long for her—at forty-two, you spend a lot of nights wondering if this will ever actually happen—and now that she’s here, I feel like I’m constantly walking on eggshells with my own heart.

Everything feels so high-stakes. I’m doing okay, I suppose, but I feel like I’m performing for an audience that doesn't exist. My midwife is wonderful, really, but when she’s here, I find myself nodding along and acting like I’ve got it all figured out, even when I’m barely holding it together. The breastfeeding... that’s the part that keeps me up. Yesterday, I just sat on the edge of the bed and cried because she wouldn't latch, and I felt like such a failure. Like I’d prepared for the pregnancy, read all the books, and now I’m failing the actual *mothering* part. It’s messy, and it’s exhausting, and I’m just trying to keep my head above water until tomorrow’s appointment. I’m nervous about that, too. I just want to do right by her.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I think, honestly, I was a bit naive. I kept thinking, "I’m 42, I’m healthy, my pregnancy was textbook—I’ll just bounce back." I had this image of myself being that person who’s up and at it, maybe taking a stroll through downtown Lowell with the stroller a week later, looking like everything was totally under control. I thought recovery would be physical—like, "Okay, the stitches are healing, the soreness is fading, check."

But the reality? It’s not just physical. It’s this weird, hollowed-out feeling. My body feels like a stranger’s—it’s not just the healing, it’s the exhaustion that reaches into your bones. I expected to feel strong because I’d *achieved* this goal, but instead, I feel incredibly fragile. Every time I move too quickly, I’m reminded of the toll it took. And the breastfeeding—nobody tells you how much that takes out of your physical recovery, too. My back aches, my energy is just gone, and the focus is so entirely on her that I feel like I’ve completely neglected my own healing. I think I expected a finish line, but there isn't one. It’s just this long, slow slog where I’m trying to put myself back together while keeping a tiny person alive. It’s much messier than the books made it sound.
Was there a physical aspect of recovery that no one had prepared you for, or that surprised you significantly?

You know, everyone talks about the big things—the birth itself, the pain, the exhaustion—but nobody mentioned the sheer, unrelenting *emptiness* of my body once she was out. For nine months, I felt her every movement, that connection was constant. Then, suddenly, she’s in the bassinet and I’m just... me again, but a broken version of me.

The thing that really blindsided me, though, was the physical anxiety. I didn't realize that my nervous system would just stay "on" 24/7. Even when she’s sleeping, I’m physically vibrating with this need to check if she’s breathing. I’ll be sitting there trying to rest, and my heart is just pounding like I’ve run a marathon. My hands shake sometimes when I’m trying to get her latched, and that frustration makes my body tense up, which makes the breastfeeding even harder. It’s this vicious, physical cycle. I thought I’d be able to just "rest when the baby rests," but my body doesn't seem to know how to switch off anymore. I feel like I’m constantly holding my breath, waiting for the next cry or the next hunger cue. I wasn't prepared for how much my body would just refuse to relax.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I genuinely thought I was prepared for this. I read all the articles, I took the class, and I really believed that because I was so motivated, it would just... happen. I pictured these calm, bonding moments—me, sitting by the window in the nursery, my daughter nursing peacefully while I looked out at the mills. I expected it to be natural, like an instinct that would just kick in the moment she arrived.

The reality hit me like a brick wall. It’s been nothing but a struggle. There’s no "calm" when you’re both frustrated, your nipples are raw, and she’s rooting and crying, and I’m franticly trying to get that perfect, deep latch I’ve read so much about. Every time she pulls away, I feel this sharp, sinking feeling in my gut. Is she hungry? Did I do it wrong? Am I not producing enough? 

I feel like a student failing a test I’ve studied my whole life for. Even when my midwife is there and she’s supportive, I just feel this immense pressure to be a "success story." I want it to be easy, and the fact that it isn't makes me feel like I’m already failing her. It’s the furthest thing from peaceful; it’s a constant, high-stakes battle.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

That’s such a hard question to put into words, but yes. The support I’ve received has been very clinical, very technical. When the midwife or the lactation consultant comes by, they’re all about the mechanics—"tilt her chin here," "support the breast this way," "check the rhythm of the swallow." And I appreciate that, I really do. I need that information. But it feels like they’re teaching me to operate a machine, not how to sit with my own daughter.

What I think I’ve been starving for is someone to just say, "It’s okay that you’re terrified. It’s okay that you’re crying." I need someone to acknowledge that my heart is breaking alongside the physical difficulty. When I’m struggling and my hands are shaking, I don’t just need a correction on my posture; I need someone to hold space for the fact that I feel like a failure. The support is all head-knowledge, and I’m drowning in my own feelings. I feel like if I admit how much the emotional weight of it is crushing me, they’ll look at me like I’m not cut out for this. So, I stay quiet, I focus on the "latch," and I just keep that wall up. It’s lonely.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I fell into the trap of believing that my age—being 42—meant I’d have this steady, seasoned perspective. I convinced myself that I was "done" with the insecurity of my twenties and that motherhood would just be this serene, fulfilling chapter. I expected to feel overwhelmed, sure, but I thought it would be a "happy" overwhelmed, the kind where you just gaze at the baby and all your worries evaporate. I thought I’d be that composed, capable mom who took everything in stride.

The reality has been a lot darker and far more frantic. I didn’t account for the sheer, suffocating weight of responsibility. I don't feel "wise"; I feel raw, exposed, and constantly on the brink of tears. It’s not just the baby blues; it’s this persistent, hum-like anxiety that follows me into every room. Every time she lets out a cry, my pulse spikes like I’m in danger. I’m mourning the person I was before—someone who could just go for a walk or sleep through the night without a second thought. I’m not just grieving the loss of my old life; I’m grieving the version of "perfect mother" I thought I’d be. It’s a lot to process when you’re barely getting three hours of sleep.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

Oh, absolutely. There is this invisible, suffocating pressure to be the "perfect postpartum success story," especially because I waited so long for her. I feel like because I’m 42 and worked so hard to get here, I’m not *allowed* to be miserable. If I complain or say I’m struggling, I feel like people might think, "Well, you begged for this, didn't you?"

With the professionals, I find myself filtering everything. When the midwife asks how I’m doing, I give her the "everything is fine" version because I don’t want to be labeled as someone who isn't coping well. I’m terrified that if I admit how truly anxious I am—or how much I cry—they’ll see me as unstable or incompetent. And with my family? It’s even harder. They look at me and see a "happy new mother," and I don't want to shatter that image for them. 

I’ve built this wall where I perform "joy" because I feel like I owe it to everyone—and to my daughter—to be the picture of gratitude. But behind that wall, I’m constantly holding my breath. I feel like I have to earn the right to be a mother by keeping my messy, frightened reality hidden. It’s a very lonely performance.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

Before she arrived, I had this vision of "integrating" her into my life. I’m a professional, I’ve had my own routine in Lowell for years, and I truly thought I’d just be "Cherie, plus a baby." I thought I’d still be *me*, just with an adorable sidekick. I didn’t think my identity would be erased; I thought it would just be expanded.

Now? I don’t even know who that woman is anymore. She feels like someone I used to read about. Everything that used to define me—my career, my independence, even just the way I occupied space in my own house—has been completely swallowed up by this new, all-consuming identity: "Mother." And honestly, it’s terrifying. I’m just a vessel for her needs right now. I’m a set of breasts, a pair of arms, and a pair of ears waiting for a cry. 

I’m struggling to find the intersection between who I was and who I’m supposed to be. I look in the mirror and I see the same face, but the eyes look different—they’re constantly scanning, constantly searching for threats. I feel like I’ve lost the "Cherie" who could handle things, and replaced her with someone who is just a collection of nerves. I’m trying to find my way back, but I’m not sure if that person even exists anymore.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

It’s the autonomy, more than anything. I’ve spent my entire adult life being someone who is self-sufficient—I’ve navigated my career, I’ve made my own decisions, I’ve been the one people come to when things need to get done. I took a lot of pride in that. I thought I could manage this transition the way I managed a project at work, with lists and research and grit.

But the "professional" part of me feels like it’s completely evaporated. I haven’t even checked my emails in three weeks, and when I do think about work, I feel this strange guilt, like I’m abandoning my daughter just by having a thought that isn't about her. And socially? I just feel so disconnected. My friends mean well, but they talk about things that feel like they’re happening on another planet—going out for dinner, talking about meetings, having a glass of wine. I’m stuck here in the nursing chair, obsessing over ounces and latches. 

The hardest part is that I don't feel like I’m "missing out" on those things, but I *am* missing out on myself. I’ve become so hyper-focused on her that I’ve let my own hobbies, my own quiet time, and my own sense of competence slip away. I’m a student again, but a failing one, and that’s a very hard identity to wear after being an expert in my own life for so long.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I suppose I expected these appointments to be my anchor. I thought of them as a safety net—somewhere I could finally set down the heavy suitcase of "I’m doing great" and actually get some real guidance, maybe even a little bit of validation that this is hard for everyone. I expected the midwife to be like a coach, helping me adjust the play when things weren't working.

In reality, the care feels incredibly transactional. It’s all about the data: "Is she gaining weight? How many wet diapers? Is your incision healing?" They have a checklist, and once the boxes are ticked, the visit is over. It’s efficient, sure, but it feels like they’re checking the health of the "unit" rather than checking in on the human beings. I leave these appointments feeling like I’ve passed a physical exam, but my spirit feels completely untouched. 

There’s no room in the standard 20-minute slot for me to say, "I’m losing my mind and I don't know who I am anymore." If I bring that up, it feels like I’m derailing the appointment. So, I talk about the weight gain and the latch, and the midwife tells me I’m doing a great job, and then we part ways. I walk out to the car feeling just as alone as I did when I walked in.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

I think the biggest one was when I tried to bring up how much I’m struggling with my anxiety. I didn't say "postpartum depression" because that felt too heavy, too "official." I just mentioned, "I feel like I’m constantly shaking, I can’t sleep even when she sleeps, and I feel like I’m living on the edge of a breakdown." 

I thought maybe they’d sit down, pull up a chair, and really *listen*. But the response was so… scripted. They just nodded and said, "Oh, that’s completely normal, the hormones are wild, it’ll pass." And then they went right back to asking about my milk supply. 

It felt like they were checking off a box on a depression screening form—a "yes/no" question—rather than hearing the desperation in my voice. It made me feel like I was just being dramatic. If the professional tells me it’s "normal," then I guess I’m just supposed to suck it up and deal with it. But it doesn't *feel* normal. It feels like I’m drowning. Being dismissed like that, even if they were trying to be reassuring, just made me shut down. I realized that if I want to be taken seriously, I’d have to be in a much worse state than I already am, and that’s a terrifying thought.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I haven’t returned to the office yet, but the mere thought of it is already paralyzing. I’m starting to think about "finding" my way back—I’m self-employed in a way, or at least my work is so tied to my daily output—and the prospect feels like tearing a bandage off a wound that hasn't even started to knit.

I expected that I’d be able to compartmentalize. I’d be "Mom" during the nursing sessions and "Cherie" when I opened my laptop. I thought I’d feel empowered, like I was reclaiming a piece of myself. But now, just anticipating that shift makes me feel incredibly guilty. How can I possibly focus on a client call when I’m still obsessing over her weight and the damn latch? 

The reality is that I don’t think I’m "ready" to be that split person. My brain is so entirely rewired for her survival that the idea of a professional deadline feels like an intrusion. I’m scared that when I do go back, I won’t be the competent person I was. I’ll be distracted, I’ll be tired, and I’ll be constantly checking the clock, wondering if she’s okay. It’s not just a practical transition; it’s an identity crisis. I feel like I’m going to be a shadow of myself in both places, and that terrifies me more than the sleepless nights.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

I haven’t officially stepped back into the office, but just the *anticipation* of it is a mountain I can’t see over. The biggest, scariest hurdle is the feeding schedule. Right now, I’m so tied to her needs that I don't even know how I’m supposed to "pump" or "find time" while keeping up with the work I’m supposed to be doing. Everyone talks about "pumping at your desk" or "working from home," but they don't explain the mental gymnastics. If I’m on a call and my let-down happens, or if I’m leaking, or if she’s crying in the other room—how am I supposed to stay focused? 

Then there’s the physical piece. I’m still so tired, and my body feels like it’s been put through a wringer. The idea of being "on" for a client, being sharp and professional, when I’ve been up all night with a crying baby... it feels like I’m going to be a fraud. I’m scared I’ll be sitting at my desk, looking at my computer, but my heart will be stuck in that nursery. I don’t know how to turn off the "mother" alarm in my head. I feel like I’m expected to pick up exactly where I left off, but I’m not the same person anymore. I’m just terrified that I’ll try to do both and fail at both.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I expected us to be a team, you know? I pictured us as this well-oiled machine: he’d handle the diaper changes while I nursed, we’d tag-team the nights, and we’d have these quiet moments of "look what we created" amidst the chaos. I thought our marriage would deepen, that we’d be closer than ever because we were finally, *finally* parents after all those years of waiting.

The reality has been… lonelier than I expected. He’s trying, he really is, but he’s still the person he was before the baby arrived, while I’ve been entirely fundamentally changed. He sleeps through her cries sometimes, and I feel this flash of cold anger, then immediate guilt for feeling it. When he does help, he asks me *how* to do things, and it’s just one more decision I have to make, one more instruction I have to give. I don’t want to be the manager of his caregiving; I want him to just *see* what needs to be done.

We’re both exhausted, but we’re exhausted in different worlds. He goes back to his work, and I’m stuck here in the middle of this nursing-crying-worrying cycle. I’m scared to tell him how broken I feel because I don’t want to ruin this, or make him think I’m regretting anything. We’re in the same house, but it feels like we’re on different planets right now.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

The biggest surprise—and honestly, the most painful one—is the "mental load" I’m expected to carry. Before she was born, we were equals, tackling projects and life decisions together, shoulder to shoulder. But since we brought her home, I’ve become the "lead" on everything involving her survival, and he’s become the "assistant." 

I didn't expect to have to *delegate* motherhood to my own husband. If I need a bottle prepped or a diaper changed, I have to be the one to notice it, prioritize it, and then communicate it to him. It’s exhausting. It’s like I have this internal, high-alert radar that never turns off, and he’s still operating on his old settings, waiting for me to tell him what the next task is. 

I feel this surge of bitterness when I see him get a solid block of sleep while I’m up listening to her breathing, or when he asks, "How can I help?" instead of just seeing the sink full of pump parts or the nursery that needs tidying. I never thought I’d be the person who feels resentful of her partner, especially when we wanted this so badly. It makes me feel like I’m failing at being a partner, too. I just want him to *be* there with me, not just be in the same house.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I suppose I expected a transition—a bridge built for me to cross from being a woman on her own to being a mother. I thought the maternity care system would be my guide, a steady hand leading me through the medical and emotional chaos. I looked at the whole journey as a path that would be paved by professionals who knew exactly how to keep me upright.

The biggest gap, looking back from three weeks in, is that the care was almost entirely physical and completely clinical. It was all about the blood pressure, the weight, the measurements, and the physical safety of the baby. But no one prepared me for the *becoming* of a mother. No one ever checked in on the woman who was trying to learn how to exist in this new reality. 

The system treats birth like a medical event that finishes when you’re discharged, but the real, heavy lifting happens in the quiet of the night, when you’re leaking, crying, and feeling like your identity has been scrubbed out. I feel like I was given a map for a physical marathon, but no one told me I’d be running it through a psychological war zone. I wasn't cared for as a *person*; I was cared for as a patient. And there is a world of difference between the two.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

I’d change the entire definition of "postpartum care." Right now, the system feels like it’s set up to discharge me as fast as possible once I’m physically stable. It’s like, once the stitches are healing and the baby is breathing, they think the job is done. 

I’d want a model that acknowledges that the real "work" doesn't even start until you’re home and reality settles in. I’d want the care to be centered around the *mother’s transition*—not just the medical stats. Imagine if instead of just a pediatrician visit for the baby, there was a mandatory, non-negotiable "check-in" for me, where someone sat down and actually asked, "How are you holding up emotionally? Who is holding you while you hold the baby?" 

I’d want that support to be integrated into the pediatric visits so I don't feel like I’m "derailing" the process by talking about my own anxiety. It shouldn’t be an extra effort to find mental health support or breastfeeding help—it should be part of the standard, seamless routine. Right now, the system feels like a series of disjointed exams. I want a system that sees the mother as a whole person, not just a biological container for the baby, and treats our mental and emotional survival with the same priority as a weight check or a blood pressure reading. It shouldn't feel like a luxury to be heard.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think... I think I just want to emphasize how much I’m mourning the loss of who I was. People tell you, "Oh, you'll love being a mother, it's the best job in the world," and while that’s true, nobody acknowledges that *that* love comes at the expense of your own peace. 

I’m sitting here, and I look at my daughter, and she is my world—I would do anything for her, absolutely anything—but I am also terrified of losing the "Cherie" who existed before this. I feel like the system, and society, expects me to just slide into this role seamlessly, like a puzzle piece clicking into place. But I’m not a puzzle piece. I’m a human being who is being pulled apart and put back together in a shape I don’t recognize yet.

I think if I could tell other women, or tell those designing this care, it would be this: stop telling us it’s "all worth it" for five minutes and just let us say, "I’m scared, I’m lonely, and I don't feel like myself." Just let us be those things without trying to "fix" it with a pamphlet or a quick, clinical answer. I want to be a good mother, but I need to be a human being first. That’s the piece that’s missing. Thank you for listening. It… it actually helps to have said it out loud.
